coredata.dc:title: An ancient plant for the synthesis of a novel carbon dot and its applications as an antibacterial agent and probe for sensing of an anti-cancer drug
coredata.dc:creator: Shahshahanipour, M.
Rezaei, B.
Ensafi, Ali A.
Etemadifar, Zahra
coredata.dc:description: An innovative, facile, low- cost and one-pot hydrothermal synthesis was developed for preparing a high fluorescence carbon dots (CDs). In this report for the first time, Lawsonia inermis (Henna) plant as a carbon source was used to produce CDs without adding any chemical reagent and it was characterized by different techniques. The as-synthesized CDs exhibit high stability under various conditions and exceptionally solubility in hydrophilic solvents such as water and ethanol. In addition, the CDs were employed as a biocompatible probe for determination of methotrexate (MTX) in the range of 0.02 μmol L−1 to 18 μmol L−1 with a detection limit as 7 nmol L−1. Highly selective and sensitive determination of MTX was carried out through the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism. It is noteworthy that, the antibacterial studies of this CDs lead to interesting results which suggested Henna CDs kill Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria like an antibiotic drug. Also, compared with the henna plant, CDs show antibacterial properties in much lower concentrations.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Henna
Carbon dots
Green synthesis
Methotrexate
Antibacterial
FRET

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimization of experimental conditions in the determination of MTX

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A) TEM image of CDs; B) DLS diagram of CDs; C) XRD pattern of CDs; D) EDAX spectrum of CDs; E) TGA analysis of CDs; F) FT-IR spectrum of the CDs; G) Raman spectrum of CDs; H) AFM cross-sections of CDs; I) AFM image of CDs; J) UV–Vis absorption spectra of Henna CDs and Henna plant solution and Photograph of CDs under day and UV light and K) The excitation-dependent emission of the CDs.

Effects of A) Long time; B) The ionic strength and C) different solvent on fluorescence intensity of CDs; E) Influence of pH on the response of the CDs to 50.0 nmol m l−1 MTX; F) Influence of amount of the CDs to 50.0 nmol m l−1 MTX on the response at pH 9.0; G) Influence of the reaction time on the response of the CDs to 50.0 nmol m l−1 MTX at pH 9.0 and 60 μg ml−1 of CDs.

A) The fluorescence spectra of CDs in the presence of different amount of MTX; B) Calibration graph for MTX determination using CDs at the optimum conditions and C) The selectivity of the sensor toward common compounds in human blood at pH 9.0. The concentration of CDs was 50.0 nmol L−1, the concentration of compounds same concentration were 750.0 nmol L−1 reported for the human blood.
Schematic of the synthesis process of CDs by hydrothermal treatment from Henna and their applications as an antibacterial agent and MTX sensor based on FRET recognition.
Comparison of the proposed sensor with some reported methods for the determination of MTX.

row.entry: Optical sensor based on chemical nanoparticles
Optical sensor based on green CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recovery test in human plasma samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Average values for three determinations ± standard deviations.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Study of the antibacterial effect of Henna CDs.

head.ce:title: An ancient plant for the synthesis of a novel carbon dot and its applications as an antibacterial agent and probe for sensing of an anti-cancer drug

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan 84156–83111, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biology, Faculty of Sciences, University of Isfahan, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An innovative, facile, low- cost and one-pot hydrothermal synthesis was developed for preparing a high fluorescence carbon dots (CDs). In this report for the first time, Lawsonia inermis (Henna) plant as a carbon source was used to produce CDs without adding any chemical reagent and it was characterized by different techniques. The as-synthesized CDs exhibit high stability under various conditions and exceptionally solubility in hydrophilic solvents such as water and ethanol. In addition, the CDs were employed as a biocompatible probe for determination of methotrexate (MTX) in the range of 0.02 μmol L−1 to 18 μmol L−1 with a detection limit as 7 nmol L−1. Highly selective and sensitive determination of MTX was carried out through the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism. It is noteworthy that, the antibacterial studies of this CDs lead to interesting results which suggested Henna CDs kill Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria like an antibiotic drug. Also, compared with the henna plant, CDs show antibacterial properties in much lower concentrations.

ce:section.ce:para: Henna is indigenous to North Africa and Asia and has been employed cosmetically and medicinally for more than nine thousand years. In traditional medicine, many parts of Henna plant have been used to treat a variety of illnesses like fever, headache, bronchitis, diabetes, and falling of hair [1]. Besides, the protection against the sun and the antimicrobial, fungicidal effects of this useful plant have been proven [2,3]. The bacteriostatic property of henna was attributed to the presence of lawsone, 2-hydroxy-1:4 naphthoquinone [4]. In the present study, Henna plant has been used as a green source in order to synthesize CDs for the first time. CDs are a good alternative for quantum dots (QDs). For many years, QDs had various applications in many scientific fields because of their superior electronic and optical properties such as the size and composition, tunable emission wavelength, large absorption coefficient, broad absorption spectrum, photostability, and brightness with quantum yields as high as 85% [5]. They are typically made from semiconductor materials such as cadmium, tellurium, and selenium, which are mainly toxic, high cost and need several steps for preparation [6]. Recently, CDs, a new type of fluorescent material have intrigued considerable interest of researchers owing possess notable characteristics compared to the QDs [7]. These novel features are vigorous photoluminescence, high resistance to photobleaching, chemical inertness and venial cytotoxicity. Moreover, as created many functional groups like hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino on the surface of CDs, they have high water solubility, superior stability and an elevated potential for functionalization with different groups for a wide range of applications, particularly in bio purposes and targeting ability [7–11]. The major advantage of CDs compared to the QDs is facile and low-cost one-step synthesis process on a large scale from plenty of carbon-containing sources [9,10,12]. The key subject for a green synthetic strategy of CDs is using natural procedures and eco-friendly solvents. In the past few years, hydrothermal carbonization as a flexible chemical route has been greatly interested in the synthesis of highly efficient fluorescent CDs in aqueous solution using various renewal and low-cost carbon sources with good quantum yield and needless to post-treatment and surface passivation processes [8,9]. These nanoparticles (NPs), based on their specific physicochemical properties, are able to stick to the bacterial membrane. The contacts occur through electrostatic interaction, receptor–ligand and hydrophobic relations. Subsequently, cause rupturing and cross the bacterial membrane. The reaction between the NPs and important cellular components, disrupt the vital actions of the bacteria and killing them [2,13]. Of course, the functional groups on the surface, fundamental properties of the CDs and the bacterial species are important factors in the toxicity influence of the CDs [14]. In this report, we have applied the green CDs for the first time to identify Methotrexate (MTX). MTX is one of the most important anticancer, chemotherapeutic drugs and an immunosuppressive in organ transplantation [15,16] MTX is widely used in therapy of solid tumors, Leukemias, bone cancer, severe asthma and rheumatoid arthritis [17]. However, it is a cytotoxic compound and acts in neoplastic cells and the other cells and tissues simultaneously [17,18]. A number of MTX side effects are cardiotoxicity, vomiting, diarrhea, myelosuppression, hepatotoxicity and hypoalbuminemia [19,20]. Accordingly, for optimization of drug dose and achieving the best therapeutic effect, monitoring the MTX levels of the patient's blood with fast, sensitive and selective methods is lucrative and required. A number of methods have been employed for MTX determination in biological samples, comprise high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [19], electrochemical method [15], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS-MS) [21] and spectrofluorimetry [22,23]. Among these techniques, fluorescence-based methods have the most interest since of their fundamentally high sensitivity, simplicity, and ease of operation. Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) is one of the most interesting diagnostic mechanisms in fluorescence systems. FRET occurs when the transfer of energy from the electronically excited state of a fluorophore (donor) to another acceptor. This is a mechanism of non-radiative decay (quenching) and its efficiency is inversely relative to the distance between the donor and acceptor and commonly takes place with donor-to-acceptor distances between 1 and 8 nm [24]. FRET-based sensors assays possess higher sensitivity and more simplicity in comparison with other chemical analysis. Since generating a measurable signal from a nanoscale contact (non-measurable), an ability which is exceeding the potency of high-resolution optical microscopy procedures like SEM and TEM [15].

In this study, for the first time a simple, low-cost, and green approach was developed for the synthesis of highly fluorescent CDs via hydrothermal treatment of Henna leaf powder. This method has various advantages, for instance, cost-effective and the renewal carbon source, effortless synthesis without the addition of any chemical reagents, one-pot step method and the production of highly stable CDs. Moreover, the Henna CDs have been used as a fluorescence probe for detection of MTX via FRET process in serum samples and its antibacterial effect was assessed a versus two pathogenic bacteria, including Escherichia coli (Gram-negative) and Staphylococcus aureus (Gram-positive).

The dried Henna leaf powder (Shahdad, Kerman, Iran) was supplied from the local market. For preparation of the Universal buffer solution, phosphoric acid, acetic acid, boric acid, sodium hydroxide and for investigation of proficiency of the nanosensor and test the potential interference, methotrexate, Rhodamine B, NaCl, tryptophan, folic acid, histidine, methionine, MgCl2, FeCl3, acetic acid, glucose, were afforded from Sigma-Aldrich. All chemicals are analytical grade and were used as received. Deionized water was used throughout the experiments.

Fluorescence, UV–vis absorption and Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FTIR) were characterized by Jasco instruments models FP-750, V-570 and 680-plus, respectively. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained on a Philips CM30 300 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurement was performed with a Bruker D8/Advance powder X-ray diffractometer. For recording the diameters and size distribution and zeta potential of the synthesized CDs a dynamic light scattering instrument (Malvern ZEN3600 A, UK) was employed. Raman spectra were achieved on a Raman Microscope (Taksan P50C0R20, IRAN). In order to study the stabilities, compositional properties and current degradation kinetics of the carbon nanoparticles, Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) measurements were exploiting using STA 1500H Rheometric Scientific device. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) was accomplished utilizing Bruker Nanos instrument (Germany). The elemental composition of CDs was studied using energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (Seron technology AIS2300 C). For purification of the CDs, Amicon Ultra- centrifugal filter unit with 10 kDa MW cutoff was utilized.

In a typical experiment first, 0.5 g of the Henna leaf powder was added to the 40 ml of distilled water and stirred till the solution becomes greenish orange. The resulting solution was moved into a 100 ml Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. after that, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature spontaneously. The large particles were eliminated from the resultant solution by centrifuging at 12000 rpm for 20 min and filtration with a 0.22-μm filter membrane and centrifugal filters (10 kDa MW cutoff) respectively. As a final point dried under vacuum for overnight for advanced characterization. While the brownish suspension of CDs was stored in a dark place at 4 °C can be stable for more than 10 months. The process of the synthesis CDs is demonstrated in Scheme 1 .

Absolute values of the quantum yields (QY) of the as-synthesized CDs were evaluated applying the following equation [9]:
Q S
=
Q R
·
I S
/
I R
·
A R
/
A S
·
η S 2
/
η R 2

In this equation, “Q” is quantum yields, “A” is the absorbance at exited wavelength, “I” is the corrected emission intensity at the excitation wavelength and “ɳ” is the refractive index of the medium used; The subscript “R” and “S” denote to reference standard with identified quantum yield and for the CDs (excited at 400 nm) solution, using Rhodamine B (quantum yield 0.89) as the reference (η = 1.36), and CDs were dissolved in deionized water (η = 1.33). For the aim of decreasing the effects of re-absorption by the sample on the observed emission spectrum, the absorbance values (A) of all samples were always controlled under 0.02.

In a typical assay, the CDs 40 μl (60 μg/ml) were diluted to 2.0 ml using universal buffer solution (0.04 mol L−1, pH 9.0) followed by the addition of the different amount of stock solution of MTX. The mixed solutions were remained static, for 10 min, and finally, fluorescence quenching spectra were recorded with the excitation wavelength of 400 nm at room temperature. The linear range of MTX detection was taken in the recording emission confines of 420–650 nm. The fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of MTX are denoted as F0 and F at 485 nm and the value of F0-F was estimated as an analytical response. The Scheme 1 shows the detection principle of MTX.

For analyzes involving human serum, 3.0 ml of blood plasma (Health Center of Isfahan University of Technology) was mixed with 2.0 ml of 10% (w/v) trichloroacetic acid and then centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min to remove proteins and large molecules. After that ten times were diluted with universal buffer (pH = 9) and filtered through a 0.45 μm Millipore filter. Toward using the standard addition method for the determination of MTX in serum samples, different amounts of standard MTX solution were spiked into prepared plasma samples. Illustrated procedure in the previous section was performed consecutively.

Antibacterial activities of the synthesized CDs were studied using two methods of disc diffusion and gel diffusion [25]. The antibacterial potential of CDs was tested against two different bacteria Escherichia coli (Gram-negative) and Staphylococcus aureus (Gram-positive). Chloramphenicol drug was used as the positive controls for bacteria and 100, 200, 300, 400, 1000 and 5000 μg ml−1 concentrations of Henna plant extract, Henna CDs and the positive control was applied. Bacterial cultures with the Muller Hinton agar surface were incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. for observation of antibacterial activity the diameters of the zones of inhibition for each of the samples were recorded in mm with Digimizer program. For accurate determinations without any errors, each experiment was repeated thrice for pure drugs, Henna plant extract and CDs with similar concentrations and the average values were considered for antibacterial activity.

In order to synthesize the CDs with maximum performance, the influence of temperatures, the concentration of the preliminary solutions and reaction times were studied. It was observed that the fluorescence intensity amplified by increasing the reaction time up to 12 h. Also, with increasing the temperature till to 180 °C and initial concentration, the fluorescence intensity and bandwidth were increased. Finally, 12 h and 180 °C were chosen as the optimum condition for the synthesis of the Henna CDs.

The henna plant contains diversified chemical compounds such as phenolic compounds (coumarins, flavonoids, naphthalene, and gallic acid) and fatty acids, quinines, steroids and aliphatic hydrocarbons which could be glycosylated [26]. These compounds possess OH, COOH, CO and NH2 groups, which have the ability to dehydrate in the appropriate hydrothermal condition. The acceptable formation mechanism of CDs is carbonization of carbohydrates, which is included hydrolysis, dehydration, decomposition, condensation, aromatization, and passivation [27–29]. In these steps, the aromatic centers are formed and eventually, the photoluminescence carbon cores are created. These types of fluorescent CDs were not “pure” carbon materials and their photoluminescence behavioral characteristics depend on the hybridization and interaction between the carbon core and surrounding chemical groups [30].

The TEM image (Fig. 1A) illustrates that the hydrothermal treatment of unmodified Henna CDs is quasi-spherical and well dispersed. As can be seen in this figure the average diameter of CDs was estimated at 5 nm. Moreover, average hydrodynamic and distribution size of the as-prepared CDs were evaluated by DLS (Fig. 1B). The results show a narrow size distribution ranging from 3 nm to 7 nm which is consistent with the TEM results.

The zeta potential of the Henna CDs in the aqueous media was −39 mV, owing to the presence of abundant hydroxyl, carbonyl and carboxyl groups on the surface, therefore indicating the negative charge CDs are extremely dispersed and stable in the aqueous solutions.

The XRD pattern of the CDs shows a single broad reflection centered at about 22°, which is similar to the graphite 002 interlayer lattice spacing and attributed to disordered carbon atoms and a poor crystalline structure (Fig. 1C) [31,32].

EDX technique was employed to identify the composition and elemental abundance of CDs (Fig. 1D). Obviously, it was found that the C content was greater than the O content, so the more prepared CDs consist of amorphous nanocrystalline carbons.

As well, TGA with the heating rate of 1 °C min−1 under air flow was used to characterize thermal stability of CDs (Fig. 1E). This study suggests that CDs were stable until about 200 °C and loss of weight seen afterward, maybe is due to degradation or loss of those species which are bonded to the CDs surface [32,33].

The functional groups of the hydrothermal treatment CDs are distinguished by FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1F). A broad and strong, distinctive absorption band of the –OH and NH is stretching, vibration mode was monitored at about 3380 cm−1. The small band at 2934 cm−1 could be identified as the stretching vibrations of CH bonds. The band at 1585 cm−1 could be assigned to the vibration band of CC and CO stretching and 1415 cm−1 asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration of COO−. Furthermore, these functional groups cause the hydrophilicity and stability of the Henna CDs in aqueous media [34].

For assessing the extent of structural defects of Henna CDs, Raman technique that is an influential and non-destructive tool was employed. Two broad peaks at around 1320 cm-1and 1580 cm−1, were observed (Fig. 1G) which are insignia to the D band (vibration sp3-hybridized) and G band (vibration of sp2-hybridized), respectively. The D band is related to carbon atoms with dangling bonds in the termination plane of the amorphous structure. For measurement, the disorder extent and fraction of sp3/sp2 carbon in C-dot, the Raman intensity ratio of the D and G band (ID/IG) was calculated. This value was 0.74, which can conclude that amorphous carbons are the major constituent parts of CDs [31]. Fig. 1H and I, show the typical AFM cross-sections and image of the as-synthesized CDs respectively. It can be seen that the CDs are well dispersed with an average diameter of 5 nm approximately. These results are consistent with the outcomes of TEM and DLS.
Also, the quantum yield measured by using Rhodamine B as the standard sample was 28.7%.

The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of the synthesized carbon dots were shown in Fig. 1J & K respectively. CDs have a strong green fluorescence under ultraviolet excitation and broad absorption spectrum with peaks from 270 to 380 nm which is completely different from the freshly Henna solution (Fig. 1J). These peaks can discriminate the presence of the typical orbitals of a π system and the extended conjugation of CC and CO bond via π-π* and n–π* transitions. The existence of the obvious tail in the visible area of the absorption spectrum is the reason for synthesized the nanosized particles and Mie scattering by them [31].

The prepared CDs displays excitation-wavelength-dependent fluorescence with red-shifted emission peaks in the range of 405 (blue) to 501 nm (green) under excitation from 320 to 440 nm (Fig. 1K). The maximum emission intensity was observed at 360 nm excitation, which is not significantly different with the maximum emission intensity at 440 nm excitation, however, the fluorescence spectrum of the CDs under 360 nm excitation has a shoulder (at ~500 nm) because of surface state emission that increased the width of the fluorescence spectrum. In another word, the dual fluorescence bands were observed and could be attributed to the core and surface state emissions [35]. The main origin of fluorescence related to the core emission (at 430 nm) that was evidently narrower than that of surface states. So the wavelength of 440 nm was selected as the excitation wavelength in following experiments. The multicolor fluorescence happens not only from CDs diverse sizes but also from a distribution of emissive trap sites like oxygen-containing groups for example carboxylate groups [32].

To explain the fluorescence mechanism of CDs can be said that since the CDs are composed of carbon core with linked functional groups on the surface or edge, the photoluminescence behavior of both groups should be introduced. Most of the CDs contain oxygen-based functional groups, thus the less than ten nanometers aromatic sp2 domains are restricted by linearly groups with sp3 structure. The fluorescent attributes of CDs are determined by the π and π* electronic levels of the sp2 situates, which are robustly confined with the band gap of σ and σ* states of the sp3 surrounding media. In other words excitons (electron-hole) in CDs have an infinite Bohr diameter. Therefore the CDs will show quantum confinement effects and fluorescence emission by possessing a non-zero bandgap [30,32].

It is well known that the photostability of a probe plays a key role in the sensitivity and feasibility of bio-applications. In this work, it was observed that the fluorescence intensity of the prepared CDs did not change significantly after 10 months (Fig. 2A). As well, to evaluate the stability of CDs for bearing ionic medium, their emissions were assessed in a solution of 0.1M phosphate-buffered saline (pH 7.0) inclusive various concentrations of NaCl. As can be seen in Fig. 2B, the intensity of CDs remained stable approximately under different ionic strengths. This behavior suggests that the Henna CDs are appropriate probes for physiological sensing.

Furthermore, the stability of CDs in different solvents was studied. The results showed that due to existing of polar functional groups of COOH, OH and NH2 on the surface of Henna CDs, their fluorescence intensities in polar solvents are stronger than in a nonpolar solvent (Fig. 2C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of experimental conditions in the determination of MTX
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve the best fluorescence response of the CDs toward MTX, some parameters such as CDs concentration, pH and incubation time were optimized.

Firstly, the effect of the solution pH on the fluorescence quenching of CDs in the presence of MTX (on the CDs fluorescence quenching upon MTX addition) was investigated (Fig. 2D). As can be seen the maximum response of CDs to MTX achieved in pH = 9.0. So, pH 9.0 was selected as the working pH through all later experiments.

The negative surface charge of CDs at low pH values decreased, so aggregation of the dispersed CDs increased. Meanwhile, at higher pH, the surface of CDs has a higher negative charge and repulsive forces between the CDs increased, so the fluorescence intensity enhances. However, at very high pH, OH– groups, inhibit the interaction between MTX and CDs.

The effect of CDs concentration on the fluorescence quenching efficiency is shown in Fig. 2E. The fluorescence quenching efficiency gently improved by the enhanced concentration of CDs up to 60 μg/ml in the universal buffer solutions, In the lower concentrations, the sensitivity of the method decreased and the signal to noise was also very low, in contrast to higher amounts of CDs, self-absorption case to the decreasing the signal.

Moreover, in order to ensure the complete reactions and recording the stable fluorescence signal, the reaction time was optimized (Fig. 2F). No considerable changes in F0-F signals were observed after the incubation time of 10 min. Therefore, pH 9.0, 60 μg/ml of CDs and 10 min reaction time were selected as the optimal conditions in the further investigations.

As mentioned in the previous sections, the synthesized Henna CDs possess the less than 10 nm, size, so they are owed a very extremely surface-to-volume ratio and a large number of atoms located on the surface. Therefore, establish effective interactions between the CDs and surrounding molecules, leading to wide changes in the fluorescence intensity of CDs [10]. Also, it was reported that CDs have photo-induced electron and energy transfer properties as an acceptor or donor [32].

Different mechanisms are reported to explain the mechanism of fluorescence quenching which is divided into static quenching and dynamic quenching. Static quenching takes place when forming a link between the fluorophore and quencher while dynamic quenching affected by collisions [36]. In this study, the fluorescence of Henna CDs was quenched by adding the MTX. Due to the structure of the MTX, the surface functional groups of CDs and media pH it can be concluded that the quenching is via the formation of strong hydrogen bonding between CDs and MTX (Scheme 1). This mechanism is recognized as FRET or non-radioactive resonance energy transfer [23]. The distance between the CDs and MTX molecules is about 3 nm and all the conditions agree well with the principles of Förster's non-radioactive energy transfer hypothesis. Information obtained from the absorption and emission spectra of the MTX and CDs show that, MTX molecules have a broad absorption spectrum that is overlapped with the emission wavelength of CDs; so, in this assay, MTX molecules act as the acceptor (quencher) of the CDs fluorescence emission (donor) (Scheme 1). Results in the produce efficient energy transfer via FRET mechanism [37].

Under the optimal conditions, the fluorescence spectra and Signals (F0-F) of CDs in the presence of MTX were measured at different concentrations of MTX (Fig. 3A). The results explored that the fluorescence quenching responses have a nearly linear behavior (R2 = 0.987) in the range of 0.02 μmol L−1 to 18 μmol L−1 (Fig. 3B). The limit of detection (LOD) using 3sb/m equation was calculated as 7 nmol L−1.

Additionally, the relative standard deviation (RSD) was 1.7% and 2.6% for five repeated measurements of (2.0 μmol L−1) and (0.15 μmol L−1) MTX, respectively. The detection limit and dynamic range of the proposed sensor was compared with the other reported methods for measuring MTX in the literature (Table 1 ). This work displayed a wide linear range and low detection limit. It should be mentioned that this sensor is based on green synthesis CDs so it is an eco-friendly and inexpensive method.

To evaluate the selectivity of the suggested sensing system, the fluorescence response of the sensor to MTX at a concentration of 50 ng ml−1 in the presence of co-existing foreign species such as tryptophan, methionine, Glucose, Dopamine, KCl, FeCl3 and etc. (all at 750 ng ml−1) were examined. As shown in Fig. 3C, the established strategy has a highly selective toward MTX detection. Except for methotrexate, all the species which tested as the interferences either cannot form hydrogen bonding with the CDs to provide close distance conditions to the extent necessary for fluorescence quenching or their adsorption spectrum do not overlap with the CDs emission spectrum, and only methotrexate is subject to fluorescence quenching conditions with the FRET mechanism. It is noteworthy that despite the structural similarity of folic acid with MTX, Folic acid has a much weaker quenching effect on the fluorescence intensity of CDs. This is due to the weaker hydrogen bonding of Folic acid Compared with MTX with CDs [23].

For testing the ability of the proposed CDs for trace level of MTX determination of human plasma blood samples, the standard method was employed. Under the optimal condition, the results for the treated serum samples spiked with determinate concentrations of standard MTX are presented in Table 2 . The recoveries indicate that this analytical procedure, with the acceptable precision and reliability, can be applied to the determination of MTX in serum samples.

Two principal, effective factors on the Antibacterial activities of the nanoparticles are characteristic properties of them and class of bacteria. Because of their small size and high surface to volume ratio, CDs can cause membrane hurt or produce free radicals and oxidizing spices lead to bacterial death [38]. Due to the bacterial cell membrane nature, there are two bacterial species, Gram-positive and Gram-negative. Gram-negative bacteria possess rather thin and few-layer cell walls, while, Gram-positive bacteria have many layers and a thick cell wall [38]. This despite the fact that Gram-negative cell walls are more anatomically and chemically more complex than the Gram-positive cell walls. The thick wall of Gram-positive cells includes peptidoglycan and teichoic acids. While the thin wall of Gram-negative cells includes a peptidoglycan layer as well as an outer membrane which is resistant to some compound permeability [39]. Therefore, the toxicity of NPs against bacteria, related to the composition, size, functional groups on the surface and the bacterial species. It is worth noting that, Bacterial cells are rich in nucleic acid, bearing negative charges as phosphate groups. These negatively charged molecules are noncovalently cross-bridged by divalent cations (i.e., Ca2+ and Mg2+); the sulfate−cations complex helps stabilize the bacteria cell wall in environments [40] and provides a barrier to CDs.

The antibacterial study of Henna CDs against Escherichia coli (Gram-negative) and Staphylococcus aureus (Gram-positive) indicates that zones of inhibition were seen in both Staphylococcus areas and Escherichia coli. But the antibacterial effects of CDs, on Gram-negative bacteria, were observed in more concentrations. This could be due to the structure of the cell wall of Gram-negative bacteria and less of their permeability (Table 3 ). However, according to pharmacological science, a reagent that can affect both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria is significant and valuable [41]. As can be seen in Table 3, in comparison with Henna CDs, Henna plant extract shows any anti-bacterial properties at low concentrations. In fact, Hana plant extract shows antibacterial properties at a much greater concentration than Henna CDs [2]. This study showed, CDs made from Henna, are a more effective antimicrobial agent than the henna plant.

Briefly, have established an easy, cost-free and one-step hydrothermal synthesis of CDs from a traditionally used plant as the new green source. These CDs exhibited intense fluorescence and high stability without using any additive for surface passivation. Also, due to the multifunctional surface groups, the new CDs are compatible with bioanalysis and soluble in the hydrophilic solvents. It was illustrated that Henna CDs can employ as a label-free fluorescent probe for selective and sensitive detection of one of the important cancer drug (MTX) in aqueous solution and significantly in human plasma serum. Moreover, the synthesized CDs were used as an antibacterial agent. Results shows, Henna CDs are good candidates for annihilating Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. Overall, all the Henna CDs features indicate that these, eco-friendly nanoparticles could be synthesized in large scale without using any chemicals harmful to the environment.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank from Center of Excellence in Sensor and Research Council of Isfahan University of Technology (IUT) and the Iran National Science Foundation (INSF) for their support.
